entry.rationale: Ohio, Deaths, 1908–1953 is the principal indexed Ohio death collection (3.5M records, images of originals). William H. Ferber was born December 1869, so he would have been 39–83 years old across this range — the most probable window for his death. Ohio maintained statewide death registration from 1867 onward; Hamilton County records are well-preserved. This search should directly answer the question if he died in Ohio before 1954.

Ohio, Death Index, 1908–1932, 1938–1944, and 1958–2007 covers date windows that complement the Deaths, 1908–1953 collection — particularly the post-1953 window (1958–2007) for the possibility that Ferber lived into his late 80s or 90s. Born December 1869, he could have lived as late as the early 1960s. This index has 7.6M records and is free and indexed on FamilySearch.

Ohio, County Death Records, 1840–2001 (2.1M records, 997K images) holds original county-level death registrations. If the indexed searches in items 1–2 return a candidate, this collection provides the original record image for full detail — informant, cause of death, parents' birthplaces, and place of burial — required to verify the match and extract all available facts.

Ancestry holds Ohio death certificate images and the U.S. Social Security Death Index (SSDI). The SSDI covers deaths from 1936 onward reported to Social Security — if Ferber had a Social Security number (mandatory for workers from the 1930s onward), his death would appear. Ancestry's Ohio death records may also include records not yet indexed on FamilySearch. Search as fallback if FamilySearch items yield nothing.

Spring Grove Cemetery in Cincinnati is where other Ferber family members are buried — his father Gerhard (died 1917), his wife Emma Becker Ferber (died 1948), and son Charles Hubert Ferber (buried Spring Grove). A search of Spring Grove Cemetery records and FindAGrave for William H. Ferber or W.H. Ferber may supply the death date and confirm burial place. Also check the FamilySearch 'Ohio, Hamilton, Cincinnati, Vine Street Hill Cemetery Index of Burials, 1851-1986' (collection 3714673) as a secondary Cincinnati cemetery source.

Ohio, Probate Records, 1789–1996 (6.99M images) covers Hamilton County estate filings. A probate case opened after William H. Ferber's death would name the decedent, date of death, and place of death, and may name heirs. This is especially useful if vital records are unavailable or the death record is damaged. Also check Ancestry's 'Ohio Wills and Probate Records, 1786–1998'.

Cincinnati newspaper obituaries are a FAN-directed source: a death notice or obituary would name the date and place of death, surviving relatives, and often occupation and church affiliation. Search the Ohio Genealogical Society Obituary Index (ogs.org), GenealogyBank Ohio newspapers, and Newspapers.com for 'Ferber' and 'William Ferber' or 'W.H. Ferber'. The family's German-American background suggests possible coverage in the Cincinnati Volksblatt or other German-language Cincinnati papers as well.

entry.description: The Ohio County Death Record (src_001, a_002) states Wm. Ferber died 11 March 1903 in Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio. Eight consecutive city directory editions in tree.gedcomx.json (sources 3BNM-TSG through 3BNM-5PW, 1903-1910) list 'Emma Ferber and Wm' or 'Emma Ferber and Wm H' at a Cincinnati address, which could imply Wm. Ferber was alive after 11 March 1903. The conflict raises whether the death record pertains to G7JB-YH6 or whether his survival into the 1904-1910 period should be inferred from the directory entries.
entry.identity_question: Does the Ohio County Death Record for 'Wm. Ferber' (11 Mar 1903, ark:/61903/1:1:F66M-8JZ, a_002) pertain to G7JB-YH6 (William H. Ferber, born Dec 1869 Cincinnati), given that city directory entries (tree sources 3BNM-TSG through 3BNM-5PW) list 'Emma Ferber and Wm' continuously from 1903 through 1910?
entry.independence_analysis: The death record (src_001, a_002) is an original Ohio county government vital registration — an entirely different source class from the city directories. The death record was created under government authority at the moment of the event; the city directories are annual commercial publications compiled by private publishers for advertising purposes. The two sources are fully independent: different creators, different informants, different institutional purposes, and different compilation timelines. The eight directory editions (3BNM-TSG through 3BNM-5PW) are NOT fully independent of one another: all are produced by the same publisher using the same listing-forward methodology, and stale entries propagate unchanged year to year unless the subscriber or the compiler actively corrects them. The eight directory citations therefore represent at most one independent determination (the first listing after Wm.'s death, which may predate the March 1903 event entirely) rather than eight independent confirmations of continued living.
entry.weighing_analysis: Three factors decisively favor the death record. First, record category and informant proximity: the death date and place were certified by the attending physician in a government vital registration — the highest-authority informant for the fact of death, certifying from direct observation with legal consequences for error. No such authoritative informant backs the directory entries; the publisher had no mechanism for verifying vital status. Second, directness: a_002 is direct evidence of death; the directory listing implies continued living only inferentially — it records a name in a commercial listing, not an attestation that the person is alive. Indirect implication drawn from a commercial listing cannot outweigh a physician's direct certification. Third, known systematic error in directory compilation: city directories were compiled 6-12 months before publication, so even the 1903 edition likely reflects data gathered before March 1903. After a householder's death, the listing persisted unless actively corrected — a correction that required action from the widow or a knowledgeable informant, neither of whom was under any obligation to contact the publisher. Emma Becker Ferber continued living at the family's Cincinnati address through 1910, so the address and her name remained current; only the 'Wm' component required removal, and it was not removed.
entry.resolution_rationale: At issue is whether William H. Ferber (G7JB-YH6) died 11 March 1903 as certified in the Ohio County Death Record, or whether the presence of 'Emma Ferber and Wm' in eight consecutive Cincinnati city directory editions (1903-1910) should be taken as evidence he survived past that date. The death record (src_001, a_002) is an original Ohio county government vital registration in which the attending physician certified the date and place of death under official authority. It names the deceased as 'Wm. Ferber,' born ~1870 Cincinnati, married, with parents Gerhard Ferber and Eva Ferber — matching G7JB-YH6's tree data on every identity point (same_person score 0.911). The city directory entries are commercial annual publications listing 'Emma Ferber and Wm' at a stable Cincinnati address; they contain no informant attesting to Wm.'s vital status. The death record is far more reliable for the fact of death. A physician-certified government vital registration is the highest-authority source for the date and fact of death; the certification is based on direct observation and carries legal consequences. The directory listing, by contrast, implies continued existence only inferentially and from a source with no verification mechanism. The directory entries most likely reflect the well-documented pattern of stale listing propagation: after William's death on 11 March 1903, Emma continued living at the family's Cincinnati address, keeping her portion of the listing current, but the 'Wm' component — which required an active correction by Emma or the publisher — was never removed and carried forward annually through 1910. This pattern (widow's continued address entry retaining the deceased husband's abbreviated name) is a recognized class of derivative-propagation error in city directory research. Emma's long subsequent life (died 7 Dec 1948, age ~78) and William's son Charles's life history (born 22 Jun 1891, died 1967) are consistent with William having died young in 1903, leaving Emma a widow at ~33. The conflict resolves in favor of a_002: this death record is G7JB-YH6's, and 11 March 1903, Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio is the established death date and place.

entry.description: materialize_facts surfaced a Birth fact conflict on G7JB-YH6: assertion a_003 (from the 1903 Ohio County Death Record) gives birth year ~1870, while the pre-existing tree fact gives December 1869, Ohio (from the 1870 U.S. Census, tree source SL2T-6WR, 'William Faerber' in the Gerhardt Faerber household). The 1-year discrepancy is consistent with known death-certificate age-estimation patterns. The preferred evidence (December 1869) is a pre-existing tree fact not yet extracted as a research.json assertion, so preferred_assertion_id cannot be set under the current schema.
entry.independence_analysis: The two birth-year values come from fully independent sources. a_003 derives from the 1903 Ohio County Death Record (src_001), in which an unknown family informant reported birth year secondhand approximately 33 years after the event. The competing December 1869 value derives from the 1870 U.S. Federal Census (tree source SL2T-6WR), recorded by a government enumerator approximately 6-8 months after the December 1869 birth when the event was recent and the birth month readily known. Different institutions, different informants, different dates of creation — the two sources are fully independent.
entry.weighing_analysis: The 1870 census-based December 1869 date carries significantly more weight. The census was conducted close to the birth event (mid-1870); the household informant was reporting a birth from within the past year, with month and year specific and freshly remembered. The death record informant (a_003) was a family member reporting a birth date approximately 33 years after the fact, secondhand — a classic memory-degradation scenario (see historical-contradictions.md: Memory degradation over time). The 1-year discrepancy (~1870 vs. December 1869) is exactly consistent with age-rounding on death records: an informant who knew the birth was 'in the winter around 1869/1870' would naturally state '1870.' This is not a substantive conflict about when the person was born; it is a well-understood approximation artifact on the death record.
entry.resolution_rationale: The December 1869 date (from the 1870 census, tree source SL2T-6WR) is preferred over a_003's ~1870 approximation on three grounds: greater proximity to the birth event, a more knowledgeable informant (household, not a family member 33 years later), and higher specificity. However, the census birth fact has not been extracted as a research.json assertion in this session, so preferred_assertion_id cannot be set from within competing_assertion_ids. This conflict does not block q_001 (which concerns the death date, not the birth date) and does not change the identity resolution for G7JB-YH6. Formal resolution requires extracting the 1870 census (SL2T-6WR, ark:/61903/1:1:M62Z-87S) as a research.json source and assertion, after which the census birth assertion would become the preferred_assertion_id and this conflict could be marked resolved.

entry.narrative_markdown:
## The Death of William Hubert Ferber (1869–1903): A Proof Summary

**Research Question:** When and where did William Hubert Ferber, born December 1869 in Cincinnati, Ohio, and resident of Hamilton County, Ohio through at least 1900, die?

**Conclusion (Probable):** The evidence strongly indicates that William Hubert Ferber died on **11 March 1903** in **Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio, United States**.

---

### Primary Evidence: Ohio County Death Record

The primary evidence is an original Ohio county death registration for Hamilton County. This record — an **original** source containing **primary information** certified by the attending physician in an official capacity — directly answers the research question.[^1]

The record names the deceased as "Wm. Ferber," born approximately 1870 in Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio, married, with father "Gerhard Ferber" and mother "Eva Ferber." Every identity anchor matches William Hubert Ferber (G7JB-YH6): surname Ferber, birth year (~1869/1870), birthplace Cincinnati, marital status (married to Emma Becker, 10 September 1890), and parents (Gerhard Ferber, GCD9-9X1; Eva Engermann Ferber, G7J1-QF2). FamilySearch's same-person matcher scored this record at 0.911 (strong match range) against G7JB-YH6. This record is classified as **original / primary information / direct evidence** of the death date and place.

[^1]: "Ohio, County Death Records, 1840-2001", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:F66M-8JZ : accessed 22 July 2026), Entry for Wm. Ferber and Gerhard Ferber, 11 Mar 1903.

### Corroborating Evidence: Find A Grave Index

A Find A Grave Index entry for "William H Ferber" — birth 28 November 1869, death 11 March 1903, burial Cincinnati, Hamilton, Ohio — corroborates the death date.[^2] This entry scored 0.972 against G7JB-YH6 in FamilySearch's matching system and is already attached to the subject.

This source is classified as **derivative / secondary information / direct evidence**: the unknown contributor could not have personally witnessed events from 1903, and the FamilySearch-hosted index is a derivative of a user-contributed memorial database. The underlying memorial most likely derives from a headstone inscription commissioned by the family or funeral home at burial — a different physical artifact and administrative process from the county death registration, making it functionally independent of the death certificate as an informant source. This independence is conditional: if the contributor sourced the date from the death certificate rather than the headstone, the two records would share an informant chain and constitute a single evidence unit.

[^2]: "Find a Grave Index", FamilySearch (https://www.familysearch.org/ark:/61903/1:1:QV2R-MD6H : accessed 2026-07-22), Entry for William H Ferber.

### Conflict Resolution: City Directory Entries, 1903–1910

Eight consecutive Cincinnati city directory editions (FamilySearch, "United States City and Business Directories, ca. 1749–ca. 1990"), covering 1903 through 1910, list "Emma Ferber and Wm" or "Emma Ferber and Wm H" at a stable Cincinnati address. Read literally, these entries might imply William Ferber was living there as late as 1910 — seven years after the death record's date. This conflict (c_001) has been analyzed and resolved.

The resolution favors the death record for three decisive reasons. First, the death record is an original government vital registration certified by the attending physician under official authority — the highest-authority source for the fact of death, created by a person with direct legal responsibility. The city directories are commercial annual publications compiled by private publishers with no mechanism for verifying a subscriber's vital status. Second, the death record provides **direct** evidence of the death event; the directory listing is **indirect** and inferential — it records a name in a commercial publication, not an attestation of vital status. Third, city directories followed a stale-entry propagation pattern: compiled 6–12 months before publication, entries carried forward unchanged unless actively corrected. After William's death, his widow Emma continued residing at the family's Cincinnati address, keeping her portion of the listing current, but the "Wm" component was never removed. Emma's long subsequent life (died 7 December 1948) and William's son Charles Hubert's history (born 22 June 1891, died December 1967) are consistent with William having died young in 1903, leaving Emma a widow at approximately age 33.

### Research Scope and Limitations

The following repositories and record types were searched to answer this question: Ohio vital death registration (FamilySearch collections 1307272, 1949341, 2128172 — full 1840–2007 range); Social Security Death Index (correctly bypassed — coverage begins 1936); FamilySearch Find A Grave Index (collection 2221801, positive); Ohio probate records on FamilySearch (no indexed records found for this subject). Cincinnati newspaper obituaries and the underlying FindAGrave.com memorial remain unsearched due to browser-access requirements in this session; search leads are logged for follow-up. A confirmed independent original source — such as a probate filing, a church burial record, or a newspaper obituary — would advance this conclusion from **probable** to **proved**.

### Tier Declaration

This conclusion is assessed at the **probable** tier. All five GPS components are substantially met — reasonably exhaustive research declared, citations complete, evidence classified and correlated, the one identified conflict resolved, and reasoning laid out for independent evaluation — but the corroborating source is derivative/secondary rather than a second independent original with primary information. The preponderance of evidence strongly indicates that William Hubert Ferber died on **11 March 1903** in **Cincinnati, Hamilton County, Ohio, United States**, and was buried in Cincinnati.

# Mentor review: proof-critique on ps_001

## What you've done well
The conflict resolution on c_001 is genuinely strong work. You correctly recognized that eight consecutive city-directory editions listing 'Emma Ferber and Wm' are not eight independent confirmations of continued living — they're one publisher, one stale-entry-propagation pattern, so at most one weak data point (Standard 46). Your resolution_rationale walks through the full four-part structure standards 48 asks for: what's at issue, what each source says, which is more reliable and why (physician-certified original vs. commercial listing with no vital-status verification mechanism), and why the less reliable version persisted (widow kept the address current; the 'Wm' component was never actively corrected). That's exactly the kind of reasoning a BCG peer reviewer wants to see spelled out, not asserted.

I also want to call out the tier call itself as good craft, not just acceptable. You had an original, primary-information, direct-evidence, physician-certified death record with a strong (0.911) identity match and a fully resolved conflict — a case some researchers would have pushed straight to Proved. You didn't. You discounted your corroborating source because it's derivative/secondary, and you were honest that two record types (newspapers, the FindAGrave.com memorial itself) were deferred rather than searched in this autonomous run. That's Standard 43 in action: follow the evidence you actually have, not the tier you'd like.

## Worth considering
Two points, neither blocking:

First, you flagged — correctly — that the Find A Grave entry (a_013) might share an informant chain with the death certificate (a_002), if the memorial contributor sourced the date from the same certificate rather than a headstone. You disclose this caveat but never quite land the plane: does your Probable conclusion depend on src_002 being independent, or does a_002 carry the conclusion on its own? It does the latter — say so explicitly in the Tier Declaration. That one sentence preempts the obvious question a reviewer will ask.

Second, the reason you're capped at Probable and not Proved is squarely the two deferred searches (newspaper obituaries and the FindAGrave.com memorial page itself) — both already have generated URLs sitting in log_008 and log_009. That's the single most valuable next step when this project moves to an interactive session: run those two searches. If either turns up a corroborating record (or a documented nil), this question comes back for conclusion-readiness re-review with a real shot at Proved.

## What would change my mind
On the independence point: nothing needs to change now — just make the dependency explicit in the write-up.

On the tier ceiling: executing the deferred newspaper and FindAGrave.com searches (URLs already generated in log_008/log_009) and getting either a corroborating hit or a documented nil result would close the last breadth gap and open the door to re-evaluating this proof at Proved.
